title: A complete operator guide for deployment, onboarding, and day-to-day use.
subtitle: Follow this guide for setup, environment configuration, first-run verification, telemetry onboarding, UI onboarding, hardening, and troubleshooting.
bullets: Setup: prerequisites, exposed ports, and the quickest safe way to boot the local stack.
Environment: the specific .env groups you actually need to understand before the first run succeeds.
Operate: what to do in the first hour after login, including API keys, telemetry, alerting, Grafana, and RCA.
Harden: the controls to revisit before treating the stack as more than a local evaluation environment.
Reference: troubleshooting symptoms, trusted source material, and direct links back to the repository documentation.

links.label: README
links.description: Architecture, quick start, and stack overview.

title: Prerequisites and ports are straightforward, but they need to be correct.

rows: Runtime
Docker with docker compose
The local reference deployment is composed of many coordinated services, not one binary.
Bootstrap tooling
Git and Python 3
Needed for the installer path and for manual repository setup.
Primary UI
Main browser entry point for operators and the place where sessions begin.
Control plane API
Main BeObservant API, docs, health, readiness, and proxy surface.
OTLP gateway
Only ingress endpoint collectors need to reach for logs, traces, and metrics.
Gateway auth
Ext_authz service used by Envoy to validate OTLP requests.
BeCertain / BeNotified
RCA and alerting services that must be healthy for their product areas to function.
Grafana proxy
Browser-facing Grafana path that depends on an authenticated session created via the UI.

title: A successful first deployment depends on a few critical .env groups.

rows: Bootstrap admin
DEFAULT_ADMIN_* and DEFAULT_ORG_ID
These define the very first account you use to log in and the initial tenant context.
Database
POSTGRES_* and *_DATABASE_URL
If Postgres credentials change, every dependent service URL must stay consistent.
Auth mode
AUTH_PROVIDER and AUTH_PASSWORD_FLOW_ENABLED
Use local for the first deployment unless you intentionally want to debug OIDC or Keycloak on day one.
Encryption and signing
DATA_ENCRYPTION_KEY and service tokens
Replace placeholders with strong unique values so service-to-service auth and secrets are not weak by default.
OTLP ingest
DEFAULT_OTLP_TOKEN, allowlist, rate limit, cache
This is what makes collector traffic both functional and bounded.
Grafana
GF_* and proxy-related values
The Grafana root URL and auth-proxy layout need to match the proxied browser path.
BeCertain tuning
quality gating and threshold values
Start with defaults until you have enough real telemetry to tune signal quality intelligently.
Proxy and cookie security
CORS, secure cookies, trusted proxy headers
Revisit these before wider rollout so browser and reverse-proxy behavior stays correct.

title: A clean first-run sequence makes the stack easier to validate and operate.

workflowSteps.label: Pick installer or manual path
workflowSteps.detail: Use python3 install.py for the quickest local evaluation, or clone, create .env, and run docker compose up -d --build if you want full manual control.

workflowSteps.label: Wait for health, not just containers
workflowSteps.detail: docker compose ps tells you what started, but the control plane ready endpoint tells you when downstream dependencies are actually usable.

workflowSteps.label: Open the UI first
workflowSteps.detail: Log in at http://localhost:5173 before attempting Grafana access, because the Grafana proxy relies on a BeObservant-created browser session.

workflowSteps.label: Create or confirm the working API key
workflowSteps.detail: A correct active key matters because it drives both OTLP token usage and the scoped context used by UI queries and alert actions.

workflowSteps.label: Verify with known-good telemetry
workflowSteps.detail: Use the included otel-agent or a generated collector config before blaming the platform for missing signals from an unverified custom pipeline.

title: These are the first workflows to complete after login.

workflowSteps.label: Set active scope
workflowSteps.detail: Open API Keys, create or inspect a key, and make sure the right scope is active before reading dashboards, logs, traces, or alert rules.

workflowSteps.label: Validate telemetry
workflowSteps.detail: Open Dashboard, Logs, and Traces to confirm data exists before attempting advanced RCA or alert threshold tuning.

workflowSteps.label: Open Grafana through the product
workflowSteps.detail: Use the controlled Grafana entry point rather than direct proxy access so the bootstrap session and permission checks stay intact.

workflowSteps.label: Create one test notification path
workflowSteps.detail: Use Integrations and Alert Manager together so at least one channel and one rule are proven before more complex incident automation is added.

workflowSteps.label: Run one RCA job
workflowSteps.detail: Use a recent time window with enough data and read the summary, root causes, anomalies, topology, causal, and warnings tabs in that order.

title: The cleanest collector pattern is one exporter per signal, all through the gateway.
code:
exporters:
  otlphttp/logs:
    endpoint: http://localhost:4320/loki
    headers:
      x-otlp-token: YOUR_OTLP_TOKEN

  otlphttp/traces:
    endpoint: http://localhost:4320/tempo
    headers:
      x-otlp-token: YOUR_OTLP_TOKEN

  otlphttp/metrics:
    endpoint: http://localhost:4320/mimir
    headers:
      x-otlp-token: YOUR_OTLP_TOKEN

service:
  pipelines:
    logs:
      exporters: [otlphttp/logs]
    traces:
      exporters: [otlphttp/traces]
    metrics:
      exporters: [otlphttp/metrics]

title: A minimal alerting rollout is rule, channel, trigger, incident, then refinement.

workflowSteps.label: Create a notification channel
workflowSteps.detail: Use Integrations to create and immediately test one webhook, email, Slack, Teams, or PagerDuty path before making it part of an incident process.

workflowSteps.label: Create or import a simple rule
workflowSteps.detail: Use Alert Manager to start with one easy-to-reason-about expression and confirm it syncs cleanly to Mimir for the correct scope.

workflowSteps.label: Confirm active alert visibility
workflowSteps.detail: When the condition is met, verify the alert shows in the UI and that the notification arrives at the configured destination.

workflowSteps.label: Work the incident
workflowSteps.detail: Open Incidents, assign ownership, add notes, and let status changes reflect the actual alert state instead of resolving prematurely.

workflowSteps.label: Refine later
workflowSteps.detail: Do not over-optimize thresholds before you have enough baseline data; first prove the workflow end to end.

title: RCA is most useful when it is treated as a corroboration tool grounded in evidence.

workflowSteps.label: Check data quality first
workflowSteps.detail: Sparse or synthetic signals can demonstrate the workflow, but the strongest results appear once logs, traces, and metrics all exist in the same window.

workflowSteps.label: Create a job for a recent problem window
workflowSteps.detail: Use the RCA page to choose the correct scope, service, and time range before submitting the job to BeCertain.

workflowSteps.label: Read the report in order
workflowSteps.detail: Start with Summary, then Root Causes, then Anomalies, then Topology and Causal, and finally Warnings so you do not over-trust one weak signal too early.

workflowSteps.label: Use evidence links
workflowSteps.detail: Treat the report as a structured evidence bundle that points you back to exact log lines, trace spans, and metric behavior rather than as a replacement for human judgment.

workflowSteps.label: Feed the next investigation
workflowSteps.detail: Carry the result into incidents and resolution notes so future RCA has better historical context and the team keeps what it learned.

title: Review these security and operations controls before wider rollout.
bullets: Replace every placeholder token, signing key, and shared secret. Do not keep generated or example values longer than necessary.
Tighten CORS, secure cookies, trusted proxy headers, and trusted CIDR settings so browser and reverse-proxy trust stays explicit.
Move secrets to a secret manager if your environment requires it, and make sure Postgres is backed up before testing destructive workflows.
Keep auth simple at first, then move to OIDC or Keycloak only after the rest of the platform is already known-good.
Delay tuning RCA thresholds, gateway limits, and every alert threshold until the basic flows are verified with real data.

title: When something feels wrong, the symptom usually points to one narrow area first.

rows: Login fails
Bootstrap admin or auth mode mismatch
Check .env auth provider values and the admin credentials created at install time.
Grafana returns 401 or behaves oddly
Bypassed authenticated launch path or proxy/root URL mismatch
Open Grafana from the UI first and verify Grafana proxy settings.
No logs or traces arrive
Wrong OTLP token or wrong gateway endpoint
Check x-otlp-token, collector exporter URLs, and gateway validation behavior.
Ready stays unhealthy
A downstream dependency is still not ready
Use docker compose ps and the BeObservant ready payload to identify the lagging service.
Data appears under the wrong tenant
Wrong active API key or shared-key confusion
Return to API Keys and confirm the selected default and target scope.
Rules exist but nothing fires
Scope mismatch, expression issue, or missing metrics
Check rule target, metric presence, and whether the dataset actually contains the signal.
RCA finishes with weak output
Not enough cross-signal activity in the chosen time window
Increase the time range or wait for richer logs, traces, and metrics before expecting strong corroboration.
Audit or admin actions return 403
Permission issue rather than backend failure
Check group inheritance, direct permissions, and the currently signed-in user.

title: Go deeper with the full architecture and operator references.
bullets: Use the README for architecture, stack purpose, service roles, and quick-start mental model.
Use USER_GUIDE for prerequisites, deployment, UI page guidance, alerting setup, RCA setup, auth modes, hardening, and common problems.
Use docker-compose.yml, .env.example, and service main/router/config files when you need exact implementation truth rather than narrative guidance.
